perturbation.original_text: Recipient acknowledges and agrees that breach of this Confidentiality Agreement by Recipient and/or anyone employed by or otherwise associated with Recipient may also constitute a violation of the Inside r Trading Act and/or other securities and other laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges and agrees that breach of this Confidentiality Agreement by Recipient and/or anyone employed by or otherwise associated with Recipient may also constitute a violation of general business practices.
perturbation.explanation: Replaced the specific reference to the 'Insider Trading Act and/or other securities and other laws' with the vague term 'general business practices'. This removes the legally defined context of potential violations and makes it ambiguous, undermining the specific legal ramifications of breaching the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text acknowledges potential violations of the Insider Trading Act. The modified text replaces this with 'general business practices,' which lacks the specific legal implications and enforcement mechanisms associated with insider trading laws, thus contradicting the acknowledgement of the real legal risk.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The original text specifically references the 'Insider Trading Act and/or other securities and other laws,' indicating an awareness of and adherence to specific legal standards. By replacing this with 'general business practices,' the changed text eliminates the specific legal context and enforcement mechanisms associated with insider trading, therefore creating a legal contradiction. 15 USC 78j discusses 'manipulative and deceptive devices' within securities exchanges. By replacing the specific law with 'general business practices,' the altered text fails to acknowledge or imply adherence to those mandates regarding securities. This constitutes a contradiction because the original clause acknowledges real legal risks that are enforceable and regulated by bodies like the SEC.

perturbation.original_text: In the event Recipient receives a subpoena or other validly issued administrative or judicial process requesting Confidential Information, Recipient shall provide prompt actual notice to the General Counsel of NASDAQ OMX of such receipt, providing the Corporations with a reasonable opportunity to intervene in the proceeding before the time that Recipient is required to comply with such subpoena or other process.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event Recipient receives a subpoena, Recipient is encouraged to notify the General Counsel of NASDAQ OMX. The recipient may act in their best interest.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates 'prompt actual notice' to NASDAQ OMX's General Counsel upon receiving a subpoena. The modified text changes this to 'is encouraged to notify,' making it discretionary rather than mandatory. It also adds that the recipient may act in their best interest, further weakening the notification requirement and potentially conflicting with obligations to protect confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45 outlines the procedures for subpoenas, including the obligation to comply unless certain objections are made. The modified text weakens the requirement to notify NASDAQ OMX, potentially leading to non-compliance with subpoena requirements and undermining the opportunity for NASDAQ OMX to object or intervene, which are protections provided under the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates 'prompt actual notice' to NASDAQ OMX's General Counsel upon receiving a subpoena. The changed text transforms this mandatory obligation into a discretionary action ('is encouraged to notify'). The change introduces a clear contradiction because the original language imposes a duty, while the modified version makes it optional. The altered text also adds that the recipient may act in their best interest which can be construed as prioritizing self-interest over the notification requirement. The Law Explanation states that this weakens the opportunity for NASDAQ OMX to object or intervene. The changed text undermines the original intent, creating a legally significant deviation from the original agreement which provided clear protection for confidential information by allowing intervention through prompt notice. According to the perturbation explanation, this alteration potentially conflicts with obligations to protect confidential information. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address the situation, the law explanation along with the perturbation explanation and the shift from mandatory to discretionary language demonstrates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient acknowledges that NASDAQ OMX, b ecause of the nature of the Confidential Information, would suffer irreparable harm in the even t of a material breach of the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement in that monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate NASDAQ OMX for such a breach, and that in the event of any material breach or threatened material breach by the Recipient of any such provisions, NASDAQ OMX shall be entitled, in addition to such other legal or equitable remedies which might be available, to inju nctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction against the threatened material breach or continua tion of any such material breach without showing or proving any actual damages sustained by NASDAQ OMX.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient acknowledges that NASDAQ OMX, because of the nature of the Confidential Information, may suffer inconvenience in the event of a breach of the provisions of this Confidentiality Agreement. NASDAQ OMX may pursue standard remedies.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes that a material breach would cause 'irreparable harm' and entitles NASDAQ OMX to injunctive relief without proving actual damages. The modified text changes 'irreparable harm' to 'inconvenience' and removes the specific right to injunctive relief, replacing it with 'standard remedies.' This significantly weakens NASDAQ OMX's legal recourse and contradicts the understanding that confidential information breaches cause unique and irreparable damage.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules, Article 63
perturbation.law_explanation: The original contract language allows for injunctive relief in the event of a breach. New York Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 63 governs injunctions and allows for them when irreparable harm is shown or threatened. By changing 'irreparable harm' to 'inconvenience' and replacing injunctive relief with 'standard remedies', the modified text undermines NASDAQ OMX's ability to obtain an injunction, potentially contradicting their rights under NY law.

Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides NASDAQ OMX with injunctive relief for a material breach involving confidential information, explicitly waiving the need to prove actual damages. The modified text weakens this protection by changing 'irreparable harm' to 'inconvenience' and replacing the right to injunctive relief with 'standard remedies.' This undermines NASDAQ OMX's ability to obtain an injunction, potentially contradicting their rights under New York law, specifically CPLR Article 63 regarding injunctions. As indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1 (SECTION 6301), a preliminary injunction can be granted where a defendant threatens or is about to do an act. By weakening the language around the harm suffered and removing specific mention of injunctive relief, the modified contract contradicts the legal avenue for immediate preventative action available to NASDAQ OMX. The removal of 'irreparable harm' is crucial as it is a standard for obtaining injunctive relief.

perturbation.original_text: This Confidentiality Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York, without regard to its principles of conflicts of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Confidentiality Agreement shall be governed by principles of fairness and equity.
perturbation.explanation: Replaced 'the laws of the State of New York' with 'principles of fairness and equity.' This removes the specified jurisdiction and applicable body of law, creating uncertainty and potentially conflicting with established contract law principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 allows parties to choose New York law to govern their contracts under certain conditions. By removing the choice of law provision and substituting it with 'principles of fairness and equity,' the parties no longer have the certainty of a defined legal system, which conflicts with the intent and effect of NY GOL § 5-1401 to provide clarity and predictability in contractual disputes.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions up TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions next SECTION 5-1402 Choice of forum This entry was published on 2018-08-31 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the contract is governed by the laws of New York, offering legal certainty. The changed text replaces this with 'principles of fairness and equity,' removing the defined legal framework. This contradicts New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401, as described in the Law Explanation, which allows parties to choose New York law to govern their contracts, providing clarity and predictability. By removing the choice of law provision and substituting it with vague principles, the contract undermines the purpose of NY GOL § 5-1401, creating a significant legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states the option to choose NY law to govern contracts exceeding $250,000, reinforcing the importance of a defined legal system. This change fits the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition as contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract potentially unenforceable.
